title: EIP-8175: Composable Transaction
description: Introduces a new EIP-2718 transaction type called the Composable transaction. Base fields follow EIP-1559 with the access list removed. Signatures are encoded as typed capabilities in a trailing RLP list, each carrying a capability type and signature type for domain separation. A payer signature capability enables native transaction sponsorship. This is a simpler alternative to EIP-8141, requiring zero new opcodes and minimal changes to transaction processing.
author: Dragan Rakita (@rakita)
laymanDescription: Creates a flexible new transaction format where features like gas sponsorship are added as plug-in capabilities instead of requiring entirely new transaction types for each upgrade. A third party can co-sign a transaction to pay gas fees on behalf of the sender, with no new opcodes needed.

endUsers.description: Enables gasless transactions where a third party pays fees, lowering onboarding friction for new users.

appDevs.description: Applications can integrate native sponsorship flows without relying on off-chain relayer infrastructure or meta-transaction wrappers.

walletDevs.description: Wallets must support constructing and co-signing the new composable transaction type with capability-based signature fields.

toolingInfra.description: Transaction decoders, explorers, and RLP libraries must parse the new capability-based payload format and signature types.

layer2s.description: Layer 2 sequencers and bridges handling L1 transactions must recognize and validate the new composable transaction type.

stakersNodes.description: Validators must accept and validate the new transaction type in mempools, including payer signature verification.

elClients.description: Moderate complexity: implement new transaction type with capability parsing, multi-signature verification, and payer-based gas charging logic.

clClients.description: Minimal impact. Consensus clients handle fork activation plumbing but no new beacon chain logic is required.

benefits: Enables native transaction sponsorship without new opcodes or execution modes.
Extensible capability model avoids combinatorial growth of transaction types per upgrade.
Supports alternative signature schemes like Ed25519 via typed capabilities.
Simpler and smaller attack surface compared to frame-based approaches like EIP-8141.
tradeoffs: Adds a new transaction type that all clients and tooling must support.
Ed25519 signature support introduces a new cryptographic dependency for the EVM ecosystem.

improveUX.description: Native transaction sponsorship lets third parties pay gas on behalf of users, removing a major onboarding barrier.
